title: Today's AI Brief: AI Has Come for Serif Fonts
summary: A look at the impact of AI on fonts and OpenAI's new data center project.
imageAlt: Image from source article for AI Has Come for Serif Fonts
imageCaption: AI Has Come for Serif Fonts
imageSourceTitle: AI Has Come for Serif Fonts

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI brief. As AI continues to make waves, it seems like nothing is off-limits. From fonts to infrastructure, AI is changing the game. Let's dive in.

First up, it seems AI has come for serif fonts. According to Wired, the backlash against AI's omnipresence has led to a quest to weed out telltale signs of its use – and serif fonts are one of the first casualties. I'm not sure if I'm ready to say goodbye to my beloved serif fonts just yet. I mean, have you seen the elegance of a well-crafted serif font? It's like the difference between a luxury car and a, well, less luxurious car.

Next, OpenAI is making big moves with its Stargate project. They're breaking ground on a 1GW data center in Michigan, which aims to expand access, create jobs, and support local communities. It's a significant investment in AI infrastructure, and it will be interesting to see how it plays out. I wonder if they'll have a 'server farm' tour like a winery tour, but instead of wine tastings, they'll offer AI demos.

Building the infrastructure for the Intelligence Age in Michigan is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: OpenAI breaks ground on a 1GW data center project in Michigan as part of Stargate, building AI infrastructure to expand access, create jobs, and support communities. The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Thermal Cameras and AI Help Ships Steer Clear of Gray Whales is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: On a sunny Tuesday afternoon in May, San Francisco Bay is busy. Container ships the size of skyscrapers deliver their wares to the Port of Oakland, tankers bear fuel, and ferries carry tourists to their hikes and commuters to their jobs at AI startups. Looking down at this marine traffic from Angel Island, located near the entrance to the bay, a group of ex... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
